title: Vital Signs — Measurement & Reporting
subtitle: Basic Nursing Care · normal ranges and when to escalate
scenario: Measuring vital signs accurately and knowing which values to report to the nurse.

steps.q: What is the normal resting heart rate range for an adult?

opts.rat: A rate under 60 is bradycardia in most adults.
The normal adult resting heart rate is 60–100 beats per minute.
A rate over 100 is tachycardia.
The accepted normal range is 60–100 beats per minute.

steps.q: What is the normal respiratory rate for an adult at rest?

opts.rat: This is bradypnea and may indicate respiratory depression.
Normal adult respiratory rate is 12–20 breaths per minute.
This is tachypnea, above the normal range.
Rates below 12 are abnormally slow for an adult.

steps.q: Which set of vital signs should the technician report to the nurse IMMEDIATELY?

opts.rat: These values are all within normal limits.
These values are normal.
A respiratory rate of 8 with an SpO2 of 88% signals respiratory depression/hypoxia and must be reported at once, especially with sedating psychotropic medications.
These values are within acceptable limits.

steps.q: Before taking an oral temperature, the technician should wait about 15–30 minutes if the client has just:

opts.rat: Ambulation does not significantly alter oral temperature accuracy.
This does not affect oral temperature measurement.

opts.text: Consumed a hot or cold drink or smoked
opts.rat: Hot/cold liquids and smoking change oral cavity temperature and can give a false reading; wait 15–30 minutes.

opts.text: Taken a scheduled oral medication with a sip of water
opts.rat: A small sip has minimal effect compared with hot/cold beverages; the classic teaching targets hot/cold drinks and smoking.

steps.q: Orthostatic (postural) hypotension is BEST described as:

opts.text: A rise in blood pressure when a client stands up
opts.rat: Orthostatic hypotension is a DROP in pressure, not a rise.

opts.text: A drop in blood pressure when moving from lying to standing
opts.rat: It is a fall of ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic within minutes of standing — common with many psychotropic medications.

opts.text: A blood pressure that is high only in the morning
opts.rat: That describes a diurnal pattern, not orthostatic change.

opts.text: A blood pressure that changes with meals
opts.rat: That is postprandial hypotension, a different phenomenon.

steps.q: When counting the pulse of a client whose rhythm feels irregular, the technician should count for:

opts.rat: Short counts are inaccurate when the rhythm is irregular.
A full minute is needed for an irregular pulse.
An irregular pulse must be counted for a full minute for accuracy.
This is too short to capture an irregular rhythm accurately.

steps.q: A client taking an antipsychotic stands up and reports feeling dizzy. BP is 122/80 lying and 96/60 standing. The BEST first action is to:

opts.text: Encourage the client to keep walking to improve circulation
opts.rat: Walking while hypotensive increases fall risk.

opts.text: Assist the client to sit or lie down and report the orthostatic drop
opts.rat: Protect safety first by lowering the client, then report the orthostatic hypotension, a known effect of many psychotropics.

opts.text: Withhold all future doses of the medication
opts.rat: The technician does not independently discontinue medication; report to the nurse/prescriber.

opts.text: Give the client caffeine to raise the blood pressure
opts.rat: This is not an appropriate or ordered intervention for orthostatic hypotension.

steps.q: A pulse oximetry reading of which value should be reported and reassessed?

opts.rat: This is a normal reading.
This is within the normal range.
An SpO2 below about 90% indicates hypoxemia and should be reported and reassessed (confirm probe placement first).
This is generally acceptable.
